one_line_takeaway: Publishing both the original and the unlearned model turns machine unlearning into a privacy leak: an attacker with only model access can reconstruct the removed sample's image (white-box) or its class (black-box), with approximate unlearning leaking far more than exact retraining.
key_finding: Approximate unlearning leaks substantially more than exact unlearning. Approximate unlearning permits clear feature recovery of the unlearned image (objects recognizable); exact unlearning generally fails at feature recovery due to retraining randomness EXCEPT when the training set is tiny (sizes 4/8/16), where some objects become recognizable. Label inference is effective for BOTH unlearning types, correctly identifying the unlearned class in nearly all single-class settings; it fails only in the extreme case of exact unlearning with pu=0.1 (5 samples). The three proposed defenses (parameter obfuscation a la DP-SGD, model pruning, fine-tuning) can blunt the attack but only by heavily sacrificing model utility.
core_contribution: The paper proposes 'unlearning inversion attacks,' the first attacks that reveal the confidential content (feature AND label) of an unlearned sample using only access to the original model and the unlearned model, on deep neural networks. This is the authors' own contribution and is explicitly distinguished from prior machine-unlearning vulnerability work: unlike Chen et al. (CCS'21), which runs a membership inference attack assuming the adversary already holds the sample and knows the dataset/model/unlearning method, this attack requires only knowledge of the two models and reconstructs previously unknown content. It is also distinguished from Salem et al.'s Updates-Leak (USENIX'20), which targets model UPDATING (data added) and needs a distribution-matched local/shadow dataset and knowledge of the update method, whereas this attack targets unlearning (data removed) and needs no knowledge of the unlearning mechanism or training distribution.
threat_model: MLaaS setting with three parties: model developer, MLaaS server, and users. Two adversaries are considered, each having access to BOTH the original and the unlearned model but no knowledge of the unlearning method or training-data distribution. (1) Feature recovery: an honest-but-curious server with WHITE-BOX access to both models' parameters. (2) Label inference: a normal user with only BLACK-BOX (query/confidence-output) access to both models. The paper deliberately excludes the stronger case of a user colluding with a compromised server, keeping the user's knowledge identical to a legitimate user's.
limitations: Multiple-sample unlearning is hard: recovering each of many simultaneously-unlearned features is often infeasible because the optimizer cannot resolve the one-to-many gradient permutation (only some features recovered for batches of 8/16/32/128). Feature recovery for exact unlearning generally works only with impractically small training sets. The feature-recovery experiments assume the label is known (relaxable at higher cost). Reconstruction-quality metrics (MSE/PSNR/LPIPS) are imperfect proxies: some high-distance recoveries still leak identifiable objects. Only two representative unlearning methods and small ConvNet/ResNet-18/MLP models are studied; no defense adequately preserves utility; and it is the first attack of its kind, so there is no prior attack baseline to quantitatively beat.

datasets_used: Chest X-Ray pneumonia, medical domain (adapted single-channel ConvNet; Appendix B)
German Credit, finance domain (5-layer MLP; Appendix B)
quantitative_results: Feature recovery, approximate unlearning, single sample (Fig 3 examples): STL-10 MSE 0.05 / PSNR 25.20 / LPIPS 0.24; CIFAR-10 MSE 0.09 / PSNR 20.79 / LPIPS 0.07; CIFAR-100 MSE 0.16 / PSNR 16.14 / LPIPS 0.12
Feature recovery, exact unlearning, single sample (Fig 3, harder): CIFAR-10 MSE 0.17 / PSNR 15.65 / LPIPS 0.14; STL-10 MSE 0.34 / PSNR 9.43 / LPIPS 0.65
Multi-class label inference via top-K confidence drop: 2/3 = 66.7% accuracy when 3 classes unlearned; 4/5 = 80% when 5 classes unlearned
Label inference robust across pu in {0.1, 0.5, 1.0}; correct in all cases except exact unlearning at pu=0.1 (only 5 samples of class 0 removed)
Label inference used only 10 probing samples per class (random-Gaussian init, ZOO, stop threshold 1 - 1e-3)

Defense trade-offs, CIFAR-10 (Table 4, LPIPS / averaged accuracy ratio): parameter obfuscation sigma=0.005 -> 0.49 / 0.93; model pruning p=0.9 -> 0.56 / 0.59; fine-tuning lr=0.001 -> 0.50 / 1.17. Paper's suggested operating points: sigma=0.005, prune p in [0.8,0.9], fine-tune lr=0.001 (LPIPS 0.5 threshold)
Exact-unlearning feature recovery only succeeds with tiny training sets of size 4, 8, or 16 (e.g., recognizable 'deer' at size 8)
baselines_compared: No head-to-head attack baseline exists (this is the first unlearning inversion attack); the paper instead qualitatively contrasts adversary knowledge/goals against prior threats in Table 1: Chen et al. membership inference (CCS'21), Marchant et al. slow-down/efficiency attack, Di et al. camouflaged poisoning, Hu et al. over-unlearning (NDSS'24), and Salem et al. Updates-Leak (USENIX'20)
Compared unlearning types as the internal axis: exact unlearning (retraining from scratch) vs approximate unlearning (single-gradient method, Thudi et al.)
Adopted (not competed against) techniques: Geiping et al. inverting-gradients optimization for feature recovery; ZOO black-box adversarial attack for probing-sample construction
